Author: Dr Puranjoy Bhattacharya
Title: Beyond the Hype: Why AI Isn’t Replacing Experts Anytime Soon
Text:
Beyond the Hype: Why AI Isn’t Replacing Experts Anytime Soon
Six months ago, Anthropic’s CEO Dario Amodei predicted that “AI will be writing 90% of code within three to six months.”
Fast-forward to today — and that claim hasn’t aged well.
A recent Yale University study found that “the broader labour market has not experienced a discernible disruption since ChatGPT’s release 33 months ago.” If AI were truly writing 90% of the world’s code, you’d expect the economy to have noticed. Yet even jobs supposedly “most exposed” to AI disruption — software development, legal research, design — show no significant change in employment.
Developers report mixed results: AI tools can help with repetitive scaffolding but often slow down complex work. Worse, automated code tends to contain more security vulnerabilities than human-written code, sometimes ten times more. So much for the era of AI writing all our code.
If you’ve used these tools yourself, say for coding, writing, or design, you’ve probably seen both sides: flashes of brilliance followed by bouts of nonsense. That tension between promise and practice is where the real story of AI is unfolding.
Untangling the Word “AI”
One reason hype persists is that the word “AI” has become a catch-all. It’s treated as a single, mysterious force accelerating toward omniscience — when in truth, it’s a sprawling family tree of evolving techniques.
Early AI systems were rule-based and brittle, relying on logical “if-then” statements. Then came machine learning — algorithms that learned from data. Later, deep learning systems emerged, capable of recognising images, speech, and patterns at scale.
Now, generative AI — the branch powering ChatGPT and Midjourney — dominates headlines. But it’s only one branch, not the trunk of the tree. It doesn’t replace older methods; it builds on them.
When pundits lump everything under a single “AI” label, it fuels the illusion that each new breakthrough erases the past. In reality, AI’s evolution branches outward, not upward — a growing ecosystem, not a straight ladder to super-intelligence.
Old Tools Don’t Die — They Specialise
Contrary to the “out with the old” narrative, older AI methods quietly thrive where they fit best.
Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) still dominate real-world vision systems — from security cameras to medical imaging — because they’re efficient, compact, and dependable. Even flashy Vision Transformers often rely on CNNs in their early layers.
Meanwhile, Random Forests and logistic regression models remain staples in financial modelling, fraud detection, and business analytics, precisely because they’re interpretable and robust.
Technology doesn’t evolve by replacement — it evolves by specialisation. Just as earlier algorithms survive by doing what they do best, human expertise persists for the same reason. Context, judgement, and adaptability remain far harder to automate than code completion.
AI as a Power Tool
AI isn’t a substitute for expertise; it’s a power tool that amplifies it.
A lawyer can use AI to summarise dense case law, freeing up hours for strategic thinking. A marketer can use it to analyse consumer data and identify trends faster. A designer can use it to generate concept variations and explore new aesthetics in record time. Each example shows the same pattern: AI clears routine clutter so humans can focus on higher-order work.
However, and critically, the promise of universal productivity gain from AI itself still has a long way to go. A recent study by METR (reported in TechCrunch) recruited experienced open-source developers to perform tasks either with or without AI coding tools. Despite expectations that AI would reduce completion time by 24%, allowing AI increased completion time by 19% instead. Developers spent more time prompting and waiting for the AI to respond, especially in large and complex codebases.
That doesn’t mean coding-AI is useless — far from it. But it does mean these tools are not yet a plug-and-play replacement. The advantage goes to those who know how to embed them into well-designed workflows, rather than those who simply switch them on and expect instant gains. As Andrew Ng puts it, “AI won’t replace people. People who use AI will replace people who don’t.” The professionals who thrive won’t be those who fear automation — but those who learn to co-create with it.
The Backfire Effect
For all its potential, AI often stumbles when treated as a drop-in human replacement. Generative models still hallucinate — inventing facts 5% to 20% of the time, even with accurate inputs. And as we pointed out earlier in the article, developers using AI coding assistants could produce up to ten times more security vulnerabilities than those coding unaided according to a recent study. It’s no wonder that Amodei’s “90% automation” prediction fizzled. These systems can churn out scaffolds, but they still need steady human hands to guide and verify them.
Consider Klarna’s experiment: the Swedish fintech announced that 95% of its customer chats were handled by AI. But behind the scenes, complex queries still landed with human agents, and customer satisfaction dipped sharply. AI didn’t replace service — it rerouted it, often less efficiently.
The problem isn’t lack of capability; it’s misalignment. AI magnifies both strengths and weaknesses. Used without context, it creates new problems faster than it solves old ones.
From Fear to Fluency
The truth is less cinematic but far more empowering.
We’re not witnessing a job apocalypse — we’re entering a skills reshuffle. Just as spreadsheets didn’t eliminate accountants but made them more analytical, AI will make professionals in every field more capable, provided they learn to wield it thoughtfully.
AI doesn’t replace the expert.
It amplifies the expert.
The winners of this new era will be those who treat AI not as a threat, but as an instrument — one that rewards curiosity, discernment, and design thinking.
The question isn’t “Will AI take our jobs?” but “Will we learn to use it well enough to make our jobs better?”
